schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch lay out the whole plan from porch to shrine; Ellicott notes the site is nowhere else so named and hears in Moriah the etymology 'appearance of Jah,' who appeared to David.
Matthew Henry expounds the chosen place — Jerusalem, Mount Moriah where Abraham bound Isaac, the place David prepared, the threshing-floor of Ornan — as a type of Christ's sacrifice.
Clarke confirms from the Targum that Moriah is where Abraham, Jacob, and David alike met God.
Gill refers the reader to his chapter introduction.

themes: the temple as a type of Christ

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch date the start to the second month of the fourth year and note the height is omitted; Ellicott reads 'in the second month in the second' as a scribe's repetition, i.e. simply Zif.
Matthew Henry fills the first three years with preparation, not delay, and conjectures a sabbatical year for both the beginning and the dedication.
Clarke explains 'the first measure' as the Mosaic cubit, distinguished from the shorter Babylonian cubit adopted after the exile; Wesley glosses 'instructed' as by David and by the Spirit of God.
Ellicott reads the clause as 'this is the foundation of Solomon' (cf. Ezra 3:11), takes 'first measure' as the ancient Mosaic cubit, and notes the chronicler drops the thirty-cubit height.

Keil and Delitzsch describe the porch as matching the house's breadth and overlaid within with gold; Ellicott argues its stated height of 120 is a textual corruption, since it would quadruple the height of the house.

Clarke argues 120 is a copyist's error for twenty (letters of meah and ammah transposed), backed by the Syriac, Arabic, and LXX; Wesley alone defends 120 as a genuine height, the porch being a kind of turret.

debates.question: Is the porch's height of 'a hundred and twenty' a genuine measurement or a scribe's error for twenty?

sides.position: a copyist's error for twenty, on the witness of the Syriac, Arabic, and Septuagint
a true height — the porch was a kind of turret rising above the house

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott identify the 'great house' as the holy place, panelled with cypress and plated with gold, carved with palm-trees and chain-like garlands; they weigh the LXX, Syriac, and Vulgate renderings of the ornament.

Keil and Ellicott read the walls as garnished with precious stones (as Solomon's Ophir cargo allowed) and confess Parvaim an unexplained name, guessing at eastern regions, southern Arabia (Farwa), or India.
Clarke canvasses the guesses — Sepharvaim, Ceylon, or blood-red gold named from bullocks — and notes the Vulgate's 'precious marble' pavement.

Keil and Ellicott record the whole house overlaid with gold and cherubim graven on the walls; Ellicott parses 'beams,' 'posts' (thresholds), and 'cherubim,' and defends the chronicler against Reuss's charge of confusion.

themes: a defence against the charge of confusion

schools.summary: Ellicott names the most holy house the oracle (Debir) and, with 1 Kings 6:20, makes it a perfect cube; both note the chronicler gives, instead of the height, the 600 talents of gold — nearly Solomon's whole yearly revenue of 666 talents.

themes: the cost against a year's revenue

schools.summary: Clarke insists 'bolts' must be meant, not two-pound nails, and reads the upper chambers as the ceiling; Wesley takes the fifty shekels as the worth of each pin, workmanship and all, and the chambers as the roof.

Ellicott parses the Hebrew, takes each nail at fifty shekels (a total would be trifling beside 600 talents), and cites Babylonian and Assyrian kings who gilded their temples; Keil simply confirms the reckoning.
Matthew Henry reads the two great cherubim as standing servants representing the angels, teaching that we worship not angels but with angels (Heb. 12:22).
Ellicott notes they were of oleaster plated with gold, finds the word 'image work' (statuary) suspicious, and suspects the original read 'woodwork.'
Wesley takes 'image work' as 'moveable work' — free-standing, unlike the Mosaic cherubim fixed to the mercy-seat — also representing the attendant angels.

Keil and Ellicott work the same arithmetic — wings of five cubits apiece, twenty in all, reaching wall to wall of the twenty-cubit shrine, the inner wings meeting at the centre — Keil noting the distributive force of the repeated clauses.

themes: wall to wall of the shrine

schools.summary: Ellicott renders the second cherub's wings 'touching the wall' and 'cleaving to the wing of the other'; Keil explains the doubled clauses as distributive, describing each twin figure in turn.
Matthew Henry sums the wings to twenty cubits, the room's full breadth, and reads the outstretched span as guardian servants filling the shrine before God.

Ellicott renders the wings 'outspreading' and the figures 'standing' ten cubits high, and reads 'inward' as 'toward the chamber' — not facing each other like the mercy-seat cherubim (Exod. 25:20); Keil confirms the standing posture and inward faces.
Matthew Henry draws the theology from the posture: standing as servants, faces inward toward the ark, they were attendants on the invisible God, not objects of adoration.

themes: the cherubim standing on their feet

schools.summary: Ellicott names the veil the Parokheth (unmentioned in the present text of 1 Kings 6:21), of blue, purple, crimson, and fine linen with woven cherubim as in Exod. 26:31; Keil makes the same identification with the tabernacle curtain.
Wesley reads the inner veil as the darkness and distance of the old dispensation, rent at the death of Christ so that worshippers are brought nigh.
Matthew Henry adds the same rent-veil typology and reads the embossed cherubim ('he caused them to ascend') as lifting the worshipper's heart upward.

themes: blue, purple, crimson, and fine linen
the veil rent at Christ's death
the darkness of the old dispensation

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott judge the pillars' stated height of thirty-five cubits a copyist's error for the eighteen of 1 Kings 7:15, 2 Kings 25:17, and Jer. 52:21 — similar Hebrew ciphers confused — the capital being five cubits.
Matthew Henry saves the thirty-five as the combined length of both pillars, each about eighteen cubits high, agreeing with the parallels.

debates.question: How is the pillars' height of 'thirty-five cubits' to be reconciled with the eighteen of the parallels?

sides.position: a copyist's error for eighteen, from confusing similar Hebrew ciphers
the combined length of the two pillars, each about eighteen cubits high

schools.summary: Both take the phrase 'in the oracle' as corrupt but repair it differently: Keil reads the word as a 'collar' (Halsreife) on the capital, Ellicott as kad-debir, 'as in the oracle,' a single-letter change; both agree the hundred pomegranates were one of four rows (400 in all).

debates.question: How should the corrupt phrase bad-debir ('in the oracle') be emended?

sides.position: read it as rabid, a 'collar' or band on the capital
read kad-debir, 'as in the oracle,' changing only one letter

schools.summary: Keil records the raising and naming of the pillars (referring to 1 Kings 7:21); Ellicott sets them in the porch, adds the lily-work of 1 Kings 7:22, and defends the chronicler against Reuss's charge of unintelligibility.
Clarke supplies the Targum's sense: Jachin for the establishing of David's kingdom, Boaz for Boaz the patriarch of Judah's royal line.
